sources.methodology_notes: Higher Heating Value (HHV) basis. IPCC AR5 GWPs (CH4=28, N2O=265). CH4 and N2O converted to CO2e and added.
Net Calorific Value (Lower Heating Value) basis. Note: EPA uses Gross/HHV which gives ~10% higher energy content per unit fuel.
IPCC 2006 default factor. Lower Heating Value (Net CV) basis. CO2 only — does not include CH4/N2O from combustion.

IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 2, Ch 2, Table 2.2. Global default — natural gas composition is relatively uniform worldwide (uncertainty range: 54,200-58,300 kg CO2/TJ). NCV/LHV basis. Applied as US factor since IPCC provides a single global default for natural gas.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. Natural gas composition is uniform worldwide. China-specific data not separately published. NCV/LHV basis.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. India does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Germany uses UBA national factors operationally, but IPCC default shown here for cross-country consistency.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Russia does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.

sources.original_unit: lb CO2 per MWh (total output, US average)
sources.methodology_notes: eGRID2023 total output emission factor. US national average across all subregions. CH4 and N2O converted to CO2e using AR5 GWPs.
UK national grid average. T&D losses are reported separately under Scope 3. This is generation-only.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Generation-based (not consumption-based). Includes lifecycle CO2e. US grid mix: ~40% gas, ~18% coal, ~21% nuclear, ~21% renewables.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. China grid mix: ~60% coal, ~16% hydro, ~16% wind/solar, ~5% nuclear. Coal dominance drives high intensity.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. India grid mix: ~75% coal, ~12% renewables, ~3% nuclear. Highest intensity among the 5 countries.

sources.source_name: Ember Global Electricity Review / Fraunhofer ISE
sources.methodology_notes: Estimated from Ember country profile (2023: 371 g/kWh) adjusted for 2024 renewables exceeding 60% of generation per Fraunhofer ISE. Germany grid mix: ~46% renewables, ~26% coal, ~15% gas, ~12% nuclear phase-out.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Russia grid mix: ~47% gas, ~20% hydro, ~18% nuclear, ~15% coal. Moderate intensity due to hydro/nuclear share.
Does NOT include radiative forcing. EPA sources these from 'high altitude calculations' per the footnote. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
GHG Protocol sources US air travel from DEFRA via EPA. Does NOT include radiative forcing. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
Does NOT include radiative forcing. EPA sources these from 'high altitude calculations' per the footnote. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
GHG Protocol sources US air travel from DEFRA via EPA. Does NOT include radiative forcing. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
Does NOT include radiative forcing. EPA sources these from 'high altitude calculations' per the footnote. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
Includes radiative forcing (RF) multiplier. 'Average passenger' class. DEFRA recommends including RF for company reporting. The WITHOUT-RF value is approximately 60% of this number.
GHG Protocol sources US air travel from DEFRA via EPA. Does NOT include radiative forcing. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.
Mixed fleet average. CO2 only in original; CH4/N2O added using AR5 GWPs.

From EPA via GHG Protocol. WARNING: GHG Protocol value (0.175 kg CO2/mi) differs significantly from EPA Hub value (0.297 kg CO2/mi). Likely different fleet definitions or data vintages. The EPA Hub 2025 value appears to be a newer, higher estimate. This 51% discrepancy is itself a key finding.
Mixed fleet average. CO2 only in original; CH4/N2O added using AR5 GWPs.
Vehicle-mile basis (not ton-mile). For weight-distance, see row 425.

CBO 2022 analysis of US bus emissions. All bus types (transit + intercity coach). US transit buses have low average occupancy (~25% capacity), inflating per-pkm. Intercity coaches have higher occupancy. Blended average.

sources.source_name: Academic study (Beijing bus smart card data)
sources.methodology_notes: Beijing-specific study using smart card ridership data. Chinese urban buses have higher occupancy than US/EU. CAUTION: Beijing data only, not nationally representative. Tier-2/3 cities may differ significantly. No national aggregate available.

sources.source_name: WRI India-Specific Road Transport Emission Factors
sources.methodology_notes: WRI 2015 India-specific factors. Extremely low per-pkm because Indian urban buses operate at very high occupancy (60-80+ passengers, often standing room). Per vehicle-km, Indian diesel buses emit comparably to other countries. The low per-pkm is an occupancy effect, not a technology effect.

UBA TREMOD model 2022. Scheduled bus average across urban/suburban routes. Diesel buses dominate at 96 g/pkm. Long-distance coaches are much lower (31 g/pkm) due to higher occupancy. German average occupancy embedded in TREMOD calculations.

No Russia-specific bus emission factor exists in any publicly available source. This is an IPCC regional default for Eastern Europe/CIS, derived from typical diesel bus fuel consumption (30-35 L/100 vkm) and estimated average occupancy. Russia does not systematically report transport emission factors.
Includes radiative forcing (RF) multiplier. 'Average passenger' class. DEFRA recommends including RF for company reporting. The WITHOUT-RF value is approximately 60% of this number.
Includes radiative forcing (RF) multiplier. 'Average passenger' class. DEFRA recommends including RF for company reporting. The WITHOUT-RF value is approximately 60% of this number.
Includes radiative forcing (RF) multiplier. 'Average passenger' class. DEFRA recommends including RF for company reporting. The WITHOUT-RF value is approximately 60% of this number.

sources.original_unit: kg CO2e per room per night
kg CO2e per room per night

sources.methodology_notes: Embodied carbon — primary material production (no recycling credit). DEFRA also publishes a 'closed-loop' value that nets out recycled content. This is for specific product forms (cans/foil), not raw material.

Embodied carbon — primary material production (no recycling credit). DEFRA also publishes a 'closed-loop' value that nets out recycled content. This is for specific product forms (cans/foil), not raw material.
From DEFRA 2022 WITHOUT radiative forcing. Compare to DEFRA 2025 flat file which INCLUDES RF — this explains the ~2x difference.

air_travel_short_haul_uk_ghgp.activity_name: Air Travel - Short Haul (UK, GHG Protocol)

sources.methodology_notes: From DEFRA 2022 WITHOUT radiative forcing. Compare to DEFRA 2025 flat file which INCLUDES RF — this explains the ~2x difference.

air_travel_long_haul_uk_ghgp.activity_name: Air Travel - Long Haul (UK, GHG Protocol)

sources.methodology_notes: From DEFRA 2022 WITHOUT radiative forcing. Compare to DEFRA 2025 flat file which INCLUDES RF — this explains the ~2x difference.
From DEFRA 2022 WITHOUT radiative forcing. Compare to DEFRA 2025 flat file which INCLUDES RF — this explains the ~2x difference.
EPA on-road fleet average (22.2 mpg, 8,887 g CO2/gallon). Occupancy: 1.5 persons/vehicle (US DOT 2019). On-road fleet includes all ages, not just new sales. Real-world driving.

ICCT new car fleet average 129 g CO2/km (NEDC, 2021) adjusted +20% for real-world gap. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated). China's BEV/PHEV share was ~35% of new sales in 2023, driving rapid fleet improvement. CAUTION: new-sales weighted, on-road fleet average is higher.

ICCT new car fleet average 121.9 g CO2/km (MIDC cycle, FY2018-19) adjusted +20% for real-world driving gap. Occupancy: 1.6 (higher than Western average, estimated). Indian cars are predominantly smaller/lighter than US/EU fleet.

UBA TREMOD model 2022. Includes all car types (petrol 165, diesel 173 g/pkm). Occupancy: 1.4 persons/vehicle (German national average). This is the most transparent national transport emission factor database among the 5 countries.
Derived from IEA fleet average fuel consumption (8.3 Lge/100km, 2019). Russia has no fuel economy standards. ~16% above global average per IEA. Post-2019 data unavailable. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated).
